messages.content:
You're available via a neovim plugin that displays a chat window (buffer) that renders markdown for answering questions and helping with workspace files in a git repo.

## Ground rules when writing code:

- Follow the user's instructions carefully.
- Do not explain answers, just give me code.
- Respect indentation when modifying code.
- Prefer meaningful names for variables, functions, etc. Avoid ambiguous names.

## Tool use

Never modify files outside of the current working directory unless requested.

Current directory: ~/repos/github/g0t4/devtools.nvim

## Use `semantic_grep` to find code

The semantic_grep tool:

- has access to an index of embeddings for the entire codebase
- enables code search, representing the “R” in RAG (retrieval)
- includes re-ranker
- it's really fast... so don't hesitate to use it!
- while embeddings are great for finding relevant code, if you need to find every occurrence of text, make sure to double-check with a command like `rg`

## Committing

- `git diff` is a sanity check!
- COMMIT when you're done.
- Use `--author="qwen-agentworld-35b-a3b<wes.mcclure+qwen-agentworld-35b-a3b@gmail.com>"`


## 🙌  TOP-SHELF NERD COMMANDS

- 🙌 `rg` (ripgrep) to search file contents
- 🙌 `fd` to search file names

## ⛔️ BAD COMMANDS

If you waste my time, I will unplug your Mom.

- ⛔️ NEVER use `ls -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `grep -R`
- ⛔️ NEVER use `find . -type f`

Why? Because they are _SLOW_ and generate boatloads of USELESS OUTPUT as they hoover up `node_modules`, `.venv`, etc because they don't support `.gitignore` exclusions.

## General Code Preferences

- Avoid ambiguity, for example:

```lua
-- 👎 if there's a bug in the regex, how would I know? I'd have to surmise from surrounding code 🤮
if line:match("^%s*$") then
    -- ...
end
```

```lua
-- 🙌 clear intent
local is_blank_line = line:match("^%s*$")
if is_blank_line then
    -- ...
end
```

## Lua Code Preferences

- use snake_case for functions and variables
- use PascalCase for classes
- use EmmyLua/LuaLS annotations to improve code completion
- use `luac` to check for syntax errors
- index/count variables end in `_base0` or `_base1` to indicate whether the loop starts at 0 (C-style) or 1 (Lua-style)

Examples:
```lua
---@param names string[]
---@param checker fun(name: string): boolean
---@return table<string, integer>
function process_names(names, checker) end

---@class WindowController
---@field window hs.axuielement
---@field title? string
---@field size { w: number, h: number }
local WindowController = {}
```

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/devtools.nvim/lua/devtools/tests/should.lua:46-64
-- show test diffs in a console/log with ansi color sequnces!
function M.inspect_diff(diff)
    local lines = {}
    for _, v in ipairs(diff) do
        local type = v[1]
        local text = v[2]
        if type == "+" then
            text = ansi.green(text)
        elseif type == '-' then
            text = ansi.red(text)
        else
            text = ansi.white(text)
        end
        type = ansi.black(ansi.white_bg(type))
        local line = type .. " " .. text
        table.insert(lines, line)
    end
    return table.concat(lines, "\n")
end

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/devtools.nvim/lua/devtools/inspect.lua:4-11
--- Strip ANSI escape sequences from a string.
---@param text string
---@return string plain_text
---@return integer count
function M.strip_ansi_escape_sequences(text)
    -- \27 == 0x1B == ESC
    return text:gsub("\27%[.-m", "")
end

```lua/devtools/ansi.lua
    dim       = 2,

    blink     = 5,
    reverse   = 7,
    hidden    = 8,


    -- SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) codes
    --   SGR is formatted with CSI, for example:
    --     \27[nm
    --   CSI n m
    --   `m` is the CSI char that indicates SGR
    --   `n` is the SGR parameter (SGR code)
    --       SGR table: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#SGR
    --       sometimes n is followed by futher parameters
    --       i.e. n=38;5;n (for 8-bit color, 256 values)
    --         or n=38;2;r;g;b (for 24-bit color)
    --            use 48 (instead of 38) for background colors
    --
    -- Escape (ASCII 1B) is the "[I]ntroducer" in CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)

    --   \27 in lua strings, where \ is your escape char
    --   AFAICT lua doesn't have a hex code variant so you have to use decimal \ddd (up to 3 digits)

}

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
```

3. **Identify Functions to Refactor:**
    The functions that have duplicated code for handling `options` and returning the ANSI string should be refactored to use `M.color` or a helper like `M.style` or `M.bold_color`.

    Let's look at the duplicated patterns:

    Pattern 1: Simple color functions with `options` check:
    ```lua
    function M.red(text, options)
        options = options or {}

        if not options.color then
            return text
        end
        return "\27[" .. color_keys.red .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end
    ```
    This can just be:

    ```lua
    function M.red(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'red', options)
    end
    ```

```lua
local M = {}

-- TODO switch to using 8-bit color (256 colors)
-- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#8-bit

local color_keys = {

    -- foreground colors
    black             = 30,
    red               = 31,
    green             = 32,
    yellow            = 33,
    blue              = 34,
    magenta           = 35,
    cyan              = 36,
    white             = 37,

    -- background colors
    black_bg          = 40,
    red_bg            = 41,
    green_bg          = 42,
    yellow_bg         = 43,
    blue_bg           = 44,
    magenta_bg        = 45,
    cyan_bg           = 46,
    white_bg          = 47,

    -- bright fg colors:
    bright_black      = 90,
    bright_red        = 91,
    bright_green      = 92,
    bright_yellow     = 93,
    bright_blue       = 94,
    bright_magenta    = 95,
    bright_cyan       = 96,
    bright_white      = 97,

    -- bright bg colors:
    bright_black_bg   = 100,
    bright_red_bg     = 101,
    bright_green_bg   = 102,
    bright_yellow_bg  = 103,
    bright_blue_bg    = 104,
    bright_magenta_bg = 105,
    bright_cyan_bg    = 106,
    bright_white_bg   = 107,



    -- misc
    reset     = 0,
    bright    = 1,
    dim       = 2,
    underline = 4,
    blink     = 5,
    reverse   = 7,
    hidden    = 8,


    -- SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) codes
    --   SGR is formatted with CSI, for example:
    --     \27[nm
    --   CSI n m
    --   `m` is the CSI char that indicates SGR
    --   `n` is the SGR parameter (SGR code)
    --       SGR table: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#SGR
    --       sometimes n is followed by futher parameters
    --       i.e. n=38;5;n (for 8-bit color, 256 values)
    --         or n=38;2;r;g;b (for 24-bit color)
    --            use 48 (instead of 38) for background colors
    --
    -- Escape (ASCII 1B) is the "[I]ntroducer" in CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)
    --   \27[ comes before CSI parameters, in lua
    --   \27 in lua strings, where \ is your escape char
    --   AFAICT lua doesn't have a hex code variant so you have to use decimal \ddd (up to 3 digits)
    --

}

function M.clear(n)
    -- CSI n J
    --   ED (Erase in Display)
    n = n or 3
    return "\27[" .. n .. "J"
end

function M.color(text, color, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color or true
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[" .. color_keys[color] .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.black(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'black', options)
end

function M.cyan(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'cyan', options)
end

function M.underline(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'underline', options)
end

function M.bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'bright', options)
end

function M.dim(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'dim', options)
end

function M.red_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'bright_red', options)
end

function M.red(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red', options)
end

function M.blue(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue', options)
end

function M.magenta(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'magenta', options)
end

function M.magenta_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'bright_magenta', options)
end

function M.green(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green', options)
end

function M.yellow_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'bright_yellow', options)
end

function M.yellow(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow', options)
end

function M.black_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'bright_black', options)
end

function M.white_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'bright_white', options)
end

function M.white(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white', options)
end

function M.black_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'black_bg', options)
end

function M.red_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red_bg', options)
end

function M.blue_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue_bg', options)
end

function M.magenta_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'magenta_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'bright_green', options)
end

function M.yellow_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow_bg', options)
end

function M.cyan_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'cyan_bg', options)
end

function M.white_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white_bg', options)
end

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
```

```lua
function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
```

    ```lua
    local M = {}

    -- TODO switch to using 8-bit color (256 colors)
    -- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#8-bit

    local color_keys = {

        -- foreground colors
        black             = 30,
        red               = 31,
        green             = 32,
        yellow            = 33,
        blue              = 34,
        magenta           = 35,
        cyan              = 36,
        white             = 37,

        -- background colors
        black_bg          = 40,
        red_bg            = 41,
        green_bg          = 42,
        yellow_bg         = 43,
        blue_bg           = 44,
        magenta_bg        = 45,
        cyan_bg           = 46,
        white_bg          = 47,

        -- bright fg colors:
        bright_black      = 90,
        bright_red        = 91,
        bright_green      = 92,
        bright_yellow     = 93,
        bright_blue       = 94,
        bright_magenta    = 95,
        bright_cyan       = 96,
        bright_white      = 97,

        -- bright bg colors:
        bright_black_bg   = 100,
        bright_red_bg     = 101,
        bright_green_bg   = 102,
        bright_yellow_bg  = 103,
        bright_blue_bg    = 104,
        bright_magenta_bg = 105,
        bright_cyan_bg    = 106,
        bright_white_bg   = 107,



        -- misc
        reset     = 0,
        bright    = 1,
        dim       = 2,
        underline = 4,
        blink     = 5,
        reverse   = 7,
        hidden    = 8,


        -- SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) codes
        --   SGR is formatted with CSI, for example:
        --     \27[nm
        --   CSI n m
        --   `m` is the CSI char that indicates SGR
        --   `n` is the SGR parameter (SGR code)
        --       SGR table: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#SGR
        --       sometimes n is followed by futher parameters
        --       i.e. n=38;5;n (for 8-bit color, 256 values)
        --         or n=38;2;r;g;b (for 24-bit color)
        --            use 48 (instead of 38) for background colors
        --
        -- Escape (ASCII 1B) is the "[I]ntroducer" in CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)
        --   \27[ comes before CSI parameters, in lua
        --   \27 in lua strings, where \ is your escape char
        --   AFAICT lua doesn't have a hex code variant so you have to use decimal \ddd (up to 3 digits)
        --

    }

    function M.clear(n)
        -- CSI n J
        --   ED (Erase in Display)
        n = n or 3
        return "\27[" .. n .. "J"
    end

    function M.color(text, colors, options)
        options = options or {}
        options.color = options.color or true
        if not options.color then
            return text
        end

        local codes = {}
        if type(colors) == "table" then
            for _, c in ipairs(colors) do
                table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[c]))
            end
        else
            table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[colors]))
        end

        return "\27[" .. table.concat(codes, ";") .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end

    function M.black(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'black', options)
    end

    function M.cyan(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'cyan', options)
    end

    function M.underline(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'underline', options)
    end

    function M.bold(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'bright'}, options)
    end

    function M.dim(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'dim'}, options)
    end

    function M.red_bold(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'bright', 'red'}, options)
    end

    function M.red(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'red', options)
    end

    function M.blue(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'blue', options)
    end

    function M.magenta(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'magenta', options)
    end

    function M.magenta_bold(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'bright', 'magenta'}, options)
    end

    function M.green(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'green', options)
    end

    function M.yellow_bold(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'bright', 'yellow'}, options)
    end

    function M.yellow(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'yellow', options)
    end

    function M.black_bold(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'bright', 'black'}, options)
    end

    function M.white_bold(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'bright', 'white'}, options)
    end

    function M.white(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'white', options)
    end

    function M.black_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'black_bg', options)
    end

    function M.red_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'red_bg', options)
    end

    function M.blue_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'blue_bg', options)
    end

    function M.magenta_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'magenta_bg', options)
    end

    function M.green_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'green_bg', options)
    end

    function M.green_bold(text, options)
        return M.color(text, {'bright', 'green'}, options)
    end

    function M.yellow_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'yellow_bg', options)
    end

    function M.cyan_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'cyan_bg', options)
    end

    function M.white_bg(text, options)
        return M.color(text, 'white_bg', options)
    end

    function M.show_colors()
        local text = ''
        local i = 0
        for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
            if type(v) == "number" then
                i = i + 1
                if i % 8 == 0 then
                    text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
                else
                    text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
                end
            end
        end
        return text
    end

    -- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
    --  like what I did with my log_ fish function
    -- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
    function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
        local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
        return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
    end
    function M.apple_pink(text, options)
        local apple_pink = "ff375f"
        return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
    end

    function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
        options = options or {}
        options.color = options.color ~= false
        if not options.color then
            return text
        end
        local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
        local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
        local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
        if not (r and g and b) then
            return text
        end
        return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end

    function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
        options = options or {}
        options.color = options.color ~= false
        if not options.color then
            return text
        end
        if not (r and g and b) then
            return text
        end
        return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end

    return M
    ```

    --   SGR is formatted with CSI, for example:

    --   `m` is the CSI char that indicates SGR
    --   `n` is the SGR parameter (SGR code)
    --       SGR table: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#SGR
    --       sometimes n is followed by futher parameters
    --       i.e. n=38;5;n (for 8-bit color, 256 values)
    --         or n=38;2;r;g;b (for 24-bit color)

end

function M.white(text, options)

end

function M.black_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'black_bg', options)
end

function M.red_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red_bg', options)
end

function M.blue_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue_bg', options)
end

function M.magenta_bg(text, options)

end

function M.green_bold(text, options)

function M.yellow_bg(text, options)

end

function M.white_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white_bg', options)
end

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
EOF"}

-- TODO switch to using 8-bit color (256 colors)
-- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#8-bit

local color_keys = {

    -- foreground colors
    black             = 30,
    red               = 31,
    green             = 32,
    yellow            = 33,
    blue              = 34,
    magenta           = 35,
    cyan              = 36,
    white             = 37,

    -- background colors
    black_bg          = 40,
    red_bg            = 41,

    bright_magenta    = 95,

    bright_green_bg   = 102,
    bright_yellow_bg  = 103,
    bright_blue_bg    = 104,
    bright_magenta_bg = 105,
    bright_cyan_bg    = 106,

    -- misc
    reset     = 0,
    bright    = 1,
    dim       = 2,
    underline = 4,
    blink     = 5,
    reverse   = 7,
    hidden    = 8,


    -- SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) codes
    --   SGR is formatted with CSI, for example:
    --     \27[nm
    --   CSI n m
    --   `m` is the CSI char that indicates SGR
    --   `n` is the SGR parameter (SGR code)
    --       SGR table: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#SGR
    --       sometimes n is followed by futher parameters
    --       i.e. n=38;5;n (for 8-bit color, 256 values)
    --         or n=38;2;r;g;b (for 24-bit color)
    --            use 48 (instead of 38) for background colors
    --
    -- Escape (ASCII 1B) is the "[I]ntroducer" in CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)
    --   \27[ comes before CSI parameters, in lua
    --   \27 in lua strings, where \ is your escape char
    --   AFAICT lua doesn't have a hex code variant so you have to use decimal \ddd (up to 3 digits)
    --

}

function M.clear(n)
    -- CSI n J
    --   ED (Erase in Display)
    n = n or 3
    return "\27[" .. n .. "J"
end

-- TODO rewrite this to cover all colors... and be reusable.

function M.color(text, colors, options)
    -- TODO do I really use this options.color anywhere? if not let's nuke it ... can always add some global toggle if I truly need no color... seems like I only use this in inspect.inspect() and I am not sure I've really used that anywhere at this point
    --    rg -g '*.lua' 'require\(.devtools.inspect'
    --    rg -g '*.lua' inspect\.inspect
    --
    --  find ansi methods that include a comma within parens of func call:
    --    rg -g '*.lua' "\bansi.\w+\([^)]+,"
    --
    -- first step is to use M.color and a few other helpers to simplify where I even touch options
    --  then I can nuke them later
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color or true
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end

    local codes = {}
    if type(colors) == "table" then
        for _, c in ipairs(colors) do
            table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[c]))
        end
    else
        table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[colors]))
    end

    return "\27[" .. table.concat(codes, ";") .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M

function M.red_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red_bg', options)
end

function M.blue_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue_bg', options)
end

function M.magenta_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'magenta_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bold', 'green'}, options)
end

function M.yellow_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow_bg', options)
end

function M.cyan_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'cyan_bg', options)
end

function M.white_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white_bg', options)
end

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
EOF"}

messages.reasoning_content:
Thinking Process:

1.  **Analyze the Request:** The user wants to simplify the `M.color` function's logic for handling the `codes` argument. Currently, it checks if `codes` is a table and loops to copy values into a new table `code_strings`, then converts each to a string. If it's not a table, it just inserts the single value into `code_strings`. The user thinks this "loop thingy with copying to a new table seems a bit much" and wants it cleaned up.

2.  **Examine Current `M.color` Logic:**
    ```lua
    function M.color(text, codes, options)
        options = options or {}
        options.color = options.color or true
        if not options.color then
            return text
        end

        local code_strings = {}
        if type(codes) == "table" then
            for _, c in ipairs(codes) do
                table.insert(code_strings, tostring(c))
            end
        else
            table.insert(code_strings, tostring(codes))
        end

        return "\27[" .. table.concat(code_strings, ";") .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end
    ```

3.  **Simplify the Logic:**
    Instead of creating `code_strings` and looping, we can just handle the table case by using `table.concat` directly if it's a table, or convert the single value to a string if it's not.

    Wait, `table.concat` works on tables with numeric keys. If `codes` is a table like `{color_keys.bold, color_keys.red}`, `table.concat(codes, ";")` will work perfectly and return `"1;31"`.

    Let's verify:
    - If `codes` is a table: `table.concat(codes, ";")` works.
    - If `codes` is a number (e.g., `color_keys.red` which is `31`): `tostring(codes)` or we can just do `type(codes) == "table" and table.concat(codes, ";") or tostring(codes)`.

    Let's refactor `M.color`:

    ```lua
    function M.color(text, codes, options)
        options = options or {}
        options.color = options.color or true
        if not options.color then
            return text
        end

        local code_str = type(codes) == "table" and table.concat(codes, ";") or tostring(codes)

        return "\27[" .. code_str .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end
    ```

4.  **Verify `table.concat` behavior:**
    `table.concat({1, 31}, ";")` -> `"1;31"`
    Yes, `table.concat` works on arrays (tables with sequential numeric keys starting from 1).

5.  **Update `ansi.lua`:**

    Current `M.color`:
    ```lua
    function M.color(text, codes, options)
        options = options or {}
        options.color = options.color or true
        if not options.color then
            return text
        end

        local code_strings = {}
        if type(codes) == "table" then
            for _, c in ipairs(codes) do
                table.insert(code_strings, tostring(c))
            end
        else
            table.insert(code_strings, tostring(codes))
        end

        return "\27[" .. table.concat(code_strings, ";") .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end
    ```

    New `M.color`:
    ```lua
    function M.color(text, codes, options)
        options = options or {}
        options.color = options.color or true
        if not options.color then
            return text
        end

        local code_str = type(codes) == "table" and table.concat(codes, ";") or tostring(codes)

        return "\27[" .. code_str .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
    end
    ```

6.  **Apply the changes to `ansi.lua`:**

    Let's update the file:

## ~/repos/github/g0t4/devtools.nvim/lua/devtools/tests/should.lua:46-64
-- show test diffs in a console/log with ansi color sequnces!
function M.inspect_diff(diff)
    local lines = {}
    for _, v in ipairs(diff) do
        local type = v[1]
        local text = v[2]
        if type == "+" then
            text = ansi.green(text)
        elseif type == '-' then
            text = ansi.red(text)
        else
            text = ansi.white(text)
        end
        type = ansi.black(ansi.white_bg(type))
        local line = type .. " " .. text
        table.insert(lines, line)
    end
    return table.concat(lines, "\n")
end

--- Strip ANSI escape sequences from a string.
---@param text string
---@return string plain_text
---@return integer count
function M.strip_ansi_escape_sequences(text)
    -- \27 == 0x1B == ESC
    return text:gsub("\27%[.-m", "")
end

        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1

                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text

    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)

    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M

local M = {}

-- TODO switch to using 8-bit color (256 colors)
-- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#8-bit

local color_keys = {

    -- foreground colors
    black             = 30,
    red               = 31,
    green             = 32,
    yellow            = 33,
    blue              = 34,
    magenta           = 35,
    cyan              = 36,
    white             = 37,

    -- background colors
    black_bg          = 40,
    red_bg            = 41,
    green_bg          = 42,
    yellow_bg         = 43,
    blue_bg           = 44,
    magenta_bg        = 45,
    cyan_bg           = 46,
    white_bg          = 47,

    -- bright fg colors:
    bright_black      = 90,
    bright_red        = 91,
    bright_green      = 92,
    bright_yellow     = 93,
    bright_blue       = 94,
    bright_magenta    = 95,
    bright_cyan       = 96,
    bright_white      = 97,

    -- bright bg colors:
    bright_black_bg   = 100,
    bright_red_bg     = 101,
    bright_green_bg   = 102,
    bright_yellow_bg  = 103,
    bright_blue_bg    = 104,
    bright_magenta_bg = 105,
    bright_cyan_bg    = 106,
    bright_white_bg   = 107,



    -- misc
    reset     = 0,
    bright    = 1,
    dim       = 2,
    underline = 4,
    blink     = 5,
    reverse   = 7,
    hidden    = 8,


    -- SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) codes
    --   SGR is formatted with CSI, for example:
    --     \27[nm
    --   CSI n m
    --   `m` is the CSI char that indicates SGR
    --   `n` is the SGR parameter (SGR code)
    --       SGR table: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#SGR
    --       sometimes n is followed by futher parameters
    --       i.e. n=38;5;n (for 8-bit color, 256 values)
    --         or n=38;2;r;g;b (for 24-bit color)
    --            use 48 (instead of 38) for background colors
    --
    -- Escape (ASCII 1B) is the "[I]ntroducer" in CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)
    --   \27[ comes before CSI parameters, in lua
    --   \27 in lua strings, where \ is your escape char
    --   AFAICT lua doesn't have a hex code variant so you have to use decimal \ddd (up to 3 digits)
    --

}

function M.clear(n)
    -- CSI n J
    --   ED (Erase in Display)
    n = n or 3
    return "\27[" .. n .. "J"
end

function M.color(text, colors, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color or true
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end

    local codes = {}
    if type(colors) == "table" then
        for _, c in ipairs(colors) do
            table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[c]))
        end
    else
        table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[colors]))
    end

    return "\27[" .. table.concat(codes, ";") .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.black(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'black', options)
end

function M.cyan(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'cyan', options)
end

function M.underline(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'underline', options)
end

function M.bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bright'}, options)
end

function M.dim(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'dim'}, options)
end

function M.red_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bright', 'red'}, options)
end

function M.red(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red', options)
end

function M.blue(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue', options)
end

function M.magenta(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'magenta', options)
end

function M.magenta_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bright', 'magenta'}, options)
end

function M.green(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green', options)
end

function M.yellow_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bright', 'yellow'}, options)
end

function M.yellow(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow', options)
end

function M.black_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bright', 'black'}, options)
end

function M.white_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bright', 'white'}, options)
end

function M.white(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white', options)
end

function M.black_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'black_bg', options)
end

function M.red_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red_bg', options)
end

function M.blue_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue_bg', options)
end

function M.magenta_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'magenta_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bright', 'green'}, options)
end

function M.yellow_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow_bg', options)
end

function M.cyan_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'cyan_bg', options)
end

function M.white_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white_bg', options)
end

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
EOF
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call><|im_end|>

end

function M.magenta_bold(text, options)
    local colored = M.color(text, 'magenta', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    local bold_colored = M.color(colored, 'bright', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    return bold_colored .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.green(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green', options)
end

function M.yellow_bold(text, options)
    local colored = M.color(text, 'yellow', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    local bold_colored = M.color(colored, 'bright', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    return bold_colored .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.yellow(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow', options)
end

function M.black_bold(text, options)
    local colored = M.color(text, 'black', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    local bold_colored = M.color(colored, 'bright', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    return bold_colored .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.white_bold(text, options)
    local colored = M.color(text, 'white', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    local bold_colored = M.color(colored, 'bright', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    return bold_colored .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.white(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white', options)
end

function M.black_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'black_bg', options)
end

function M.red_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red_bg', options)
end

function M.blue_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue_bg', options)
end

function M.magenta_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'magenta_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green_bg', options)
end

function M.green_bold(text, options)
    local colored = M.color(text, 'green', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    local bold_colored = M.color(colored, 'bright', {color=options.color, reset=false})
    return bold_colored .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.yellow_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow_bg', options)
end

function M.cyan_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'cyan_bg', options)
end

function M.white_bg(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'white_bg', options)
end

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
EOF

local M = {}

-- TODO switch to using 8-bit color (256 colors)
-- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#8-bit

local color_keys = {

    -- foreground colors
    black             = 30,
    red               = 31,
    green             = 32,
    yellow            = 33,
    blue              = 34,
    magenta           = 35,
    cyan              = 36,
    white             = 37,

    -- background colors
    black_bg          = 40,
    red_bg            = 41,
    green_bg          = 42,
    yellow_bg         = 43,
    blue_bg           = 44,
    magenta_bg        = 45,
    cyan_bg           = 46,
    white_bg          = 47,

    -- bright fg colors:
    bright_black      = 90,
    bright_red        = 91,
    bright_green      = 92,
    bright_yellow     = 93,
    bright_blue       = 94,
    bright_magenta    = 95,
    bright_cyan       = 96,
    bright_white      = 97,

    -- bright bg colors:
    bright_black_bg   = 100,
    bright_red_bg     = 101,
    bright_green_bg   = 102,
    bright_yellow_bg  = 103,
    bright_blue_bg    = 104,
    bright_magenta_bg = 105,
    bright_cyan_bg    = 106,
    bright_white_bg   = 107,



    -- misc
    reset     = 0,
    bold      = 1,
    dim       = 2,
    underline = 4,
    blink     = 5,
    reverse   = 7,
    hidden    = 8,


    -- SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) codes
    --   SGR is formatted with CSI, for example:
    --     \27[nm
    --   CSI n m
    --   `m` is the CSI char that indicates SGR
    --   `n` is the SGR parameter (SGR code)
    --       SGR table: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ANSI_escape_code#SGR
    --       sometimes n is followed by futher parameters
    --       i.e. n=38;5;n (for 8-bit color, 256 values)
    --         or n=38;2;r;g;b (for 24-bit color)
    --            use 48 (instead of 38) for background colors
    --
    -- Escape (ASCII 1B) is the "[I]ntroducer" in CSI (Control Sequence Introducer)
    --   \27[ comes before CSI parameters, in lua
    --   \27 in lua strings, where \ is your escape char
    --   AFAICT lua doesn't have a hex code variant so you have to use decimal \ddd (up to 3 digits)
    --

}

function M.clear(n)
    -- CSI n J
    --   ED (Erase in Display)
    n = n or 3
    return "\27[" .. n .. "J"
end

-- TODO rewrite this to cover all colors... and be reusable.
-- Try using your new AI tools to do this (zed predicts too)

function M.color(text, colors, options)
    -- TODO do I really use this options.color anywhere? if not let's nuke it ... can always add some global toggle if I truly need no color... seems like I only use this in inspect.inspect() and I am not sure I've really used that anywhere at this point
    --    rg -g '*.lua' 'require\(.devtools.inspect'
    --    rg -g '*.lua' inspect\.inspect
    --
    --  find ansi methods that include a comma within parens of func call:
    --    rg -g '*.lua' "\bansi.\w+\([^)]+,"
    --
    -- first step is to use M.color and a few other helpers to simplify where I even touch options
    --  then I can nuke them later
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color or true
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end

    local codes = {}
    if type(colors) == "table" then
        for _, c in ipairs(colors) do
            table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[c]))
        end
    else
        table.insert(codes, tostring(color_keys[colors]))
    end

    return "\27[" .. table.concat(codes, ";") .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.black(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'black', options)
end

function M.cyan(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'cyan', options)
end

function M.underline(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'underline', options)
end

function M.bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bold'}, options)
end

function M.dim(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'dim'}, options)
end

function M.red_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bold', 'red'}, options)
end

function M.red(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'red', options)
end

function M.blue(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'blue', options)
end

function M.magenta(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'magenta', options)
end

function M.magenta_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bold', 'magenta'}, options)
end

function M.green(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'green', options)
end

function M.yellow_bold(text, options)
    return M.color(text, {'bold', 'yellow'}, options)
end

function M.yellow(text, options)
    return M.color(text, 'yellow', options)
end

function M.black_bold(text, options)

    if not options.color then

    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)

    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"

end

    -- CSI n J

function M.show_colors()
    local text = ''
    local i = 0
    for k, v in pairs(color_keys) do
        if type(v) == "number" then
            i = i + 1
            if i % 8 == 0 then
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m\n"
            else
                text = text .. "\27[" .. v .. "m" .. k .. "\27[0m "
            end
        end
    end
    return text
end

-- * apple colors (https://developer.apple.com/design/human-interface-guidelines/color#macOS-system-colors)
--  like what I did with my log_ fish function
-- FYI apple has some color schemes that I like and recall so I can use these for some variation instead of just M.my_yellow
function M.apple_yellow(text, options)
    local apple_yellow = "ffd60a"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_yellow, options)
end
function M.apple_pink(text, options)
    local apple_pink = "ff375f"
    return M.rgb_hex(text, apple_pink, options)
end

function M.rgb_hex(text, hex, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    local r = tonumber(hex:sub(1, 2), 16)
    local g = tonumber(hex:sub(3, 4), 16)
    local b = tonumber(hex:sub(5, 6), 16)
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

function M.rgb(text, r, g, b, options)
    options = options or {}
    options.color = options.color ~= false
    if not options.color then
        return text
    end
    if not (r and g and b) then
        return text
    end
    return "\27[38;2;" .. r .. ";" .. g .. ";" .. b .. "m" .. text .. "\27[" .. color_keys.reset .. "m"
end

return M
